Problem: A train company is looking into creating a software that helps on the planning of resources for mantainance, the goal is to be able to take "Requests" data and fill them with "resources" efficiently such that parameters from the user are used to find feasible solutions. Important to notice: 

Lineal solving is not the only thing needed. The user could upload as well shifts already covered "which supposes shifts that are already using resources", this means that some part of the resources will have blocked days on a calendar and it will affect other parameters, what is expected then is that the backend can run the solver considering this blockers. 

In the same way a user could block certain planned shifts after the solver with OR tools runs once, then re-run it, but respecting already blocked dates that the user liked.   

Problem extra info: This is an optimization problem that will be solved using integer programming and the OR-tools from google. For this to work, the constrains and Objectives have to be modeled as mathematical equations. Do a quick search about Integer porgramming and Google OR tools to get more context, then store that in memory, maybe isnide .claude in this folder CFF is a good idea? would that context be picked up automatically? We are looking at a one to many relation for both region and teams. GET endpoints:
  - /api/v1/resources/ - retrieves a complete list of resources in teh database. This corresponds to employees with qualifications, teams and assigned regions. It is important info that the user may want to explore. 
  - /api/v1/requests/ - Retrieves requests or also known as demands. This demands contain the specification of a job that is needed, it contains regions and qualificaitons needed. The information is necessary to match it with resources. 
  - /api/v1/assignments/ - This retrievs already assigned requests, with details about the resources ID added. 

  POST endpoints:
  - /api/v1/resources/ - Creates new resources, it may be needed to complete planning or assist the user creating a new resource. 
  - /api/v1/requests/ - Creates new requests
  - /api/v1/assignments/ - Creates an assignment

  DELETE endpoint:
  - /api/v1/assignments/{assignment_id} - deletes a specifric assignemnt.

please read this document: "C:\Users\Javie\Downloads\Bewerbung_PrototypFund_V3_Pia.docx"

That is a document describing a project. Please create a highly modern looking app as a Proof of Concept only. Only create an HTML named frontend_draft.html. This points have changed: 4, 9, 15 & 16

Here is the new paraggraphs:

Technische Umsetzung: 
 
Unser Open-Source-Prototyp unterstützt KMU bei der Vorbereitung auf Umwelt- und Nachhaltigkeitsbewertungen (z.B. EcoVadis). In der ersten Förderphase fokussieren wir auf einen klaren Umwelt-Use-Case: Ein KMU ohne schriftliche Umweltdokumente möchte eine erste To-do-Liste sowie Basisdokumente erstellen.
Die Anwendung wird als webbasierte Plattform mit React im Frontend und Python/FastAPI im Backend umgesetzt; PostgreSQL speichert Antworten, Aufgaben und Dokumententexte. Ein RAG-System mit docling ermöglicht dem Agenten, Compliance-Dokumente (Standards, Guidelines, Zertifizierungsanforderungen als PDFs) kontextbezogen zu nutzen.
Ein lokal laufender KI-Dialogassistent (Google ADK mit Gemma 3:12B, mindestens 8B) führt Nutzer*innen durch gezielte Fragen zur aktuellen Situation und erklärt Anforderungen. Der multimodale Agent verarbeitet Text- und Bildeingaben. Bei begrenzten Hardware-Ressourcen unterstützt die Plattform optional API-basierte Modelle. Aus den Antworten generiert ImpactPath/ESG-Hub eine priorisierte To-do-Liste sowie Textentwürfe für Kerndokumente wie eine Umweltleitlinie, die manuell geprüft werden. Entscheidungen werden nicht automatisiert.
Die containerbasierte Bereitstellung (Docker) ermöglicht Self-Hosting und Datensparsamkeit. Die modulare Architektur erlaubt spätere Erweiterungen: In der Second Stage wird die Verarbeitung von Nutzerdokumenten sowie Social- und Governance-Module ergänzt.

Milestones Stage 1: 
Monat 1: Entwicklung: RAG-Infrastruktur (docling), Backend-Grundgerüst (FastAPI, PostgreSQL), Datenmodell. Fachexpertise: Kuratierung von Compliance-Dokumenten, Fragenkatalog für Umweltmodul, Definition der Dokumententypen.
Monat 2: Backend-Vervollständigung (REST-API, Datenstrukturen), RAG-Pipeline-Tests. Frontend-Basisansicht (React). Fachexpertise: Entwurf der Dokumentvorlagen (Umweltleitlinie), Qualitätsprüfung der Inhalte.
Monat 3: Integration des KI-Dialogassistenten (Google ADK + Gemma) mit RAG-System. Tests multimodaler Fähigkeiten (Text, Bilder als Beta). Implementierung der To-do-Engine. Fachexpertise: Validierung der KI-Erklärungen auf Compliance-Richtigkeit, Erstellung von Test-Szenarien.
Monat 4: Frontend-Integration, vollständiger Dialogflow, Dokumentengenerierung. Fachexpertise: Qualitätssicherung der generierten Texte, Anpassung der Templates. Interne Tests mit Beispielszenarien.
Monat 5: Pilot-Tests mit 1-2 KMU, Feedback-Auswertung, Verbesserungen von Dialog, Textqualität und To-do-Logik. Exportfunktionen (PDF/Markdown).
Monat 6: Stabilisierung, technische Dokumentation, Docker-Deployment, Open-Source-Veröffentlichung.

Stage 2:
 
In der Second Stage möchten wir ImpactPath/ESG-Hub technisch und fachlich erweitern, um die Praxistauglichkeit und Akzeptanz bei KMU zu erhöhen. Da die meisten ESG-Bewertungen sowohl Umwelt- als auch Sozialaspekte verlangen, ist ein zweites Modul für den Bereich "Social" (Arbeits- und Menschenrechte) notwendig, um als ernsthafte Lösung wahrgenommen zu werden. Beide Module bieten grundlegende Einstiegshilfen, Fragenkataloge und Dokumententwürfe.
Technisch soll der KI-Agent "memory-enhanced" werden, um die Benutzererfahrung signifikant zu verbessern – Nutzer*innen müssen nicht wiederholt dieselben Informationen eingeben. Zudem ist die Verarbeitung von Dokumenten (PDFs, Scans, einfache strukturierte Daten) eine grundlegende Nutzeranforderung: KMU haben oft bereits einzelne Dokumente (z.B. Rechnungen, bestehende Richtlinien) und benötigen Hilfe, diese in den ESG-Kontext einzuordnen. Der Agent soll diese analysieren und für Dialog, To-do-Generierung und Dokumententwürfe nutzbar machen.
Parallel liegt der Schwerpunkt auf Community-Aufbau (KMU, Beratende, Kammern), Verbesserung von Onboarding und Dokumentation sowie der Klärung einer tragfähigen organisatorischen Basis (Verein, Fördermodell), damit ImpactPath/ESG-Hub über die Förderung hinaus weiterentwickelt werden kann.

Monat 1: Konzeption und Implementierung der Memory-Layer und Dokumentenpipeline (PDFs, Scans mit Open-Source-Bibliotheken). Fachexpertise: Entwicklung des Fragenkatalogs und Test-Szenarien für Social-Modul (Arbeits- und Menschenrechte), Auswahl typischer Dokumenttypen.
Monat 2: Integration der Dokumentverarbeitung in den Dialogfluss (Nutzung hochgeladener Dokumente für Erklärungen und To-dos). Fachexpertise: Entwurf der Social-Modul-Dokumentvorlagen (z.B. Richtlinie zu Arbeitsbedingungen), erste Tests mit Pilot-KMU.
Monat 3: Implementierung des Social-Moduls (Fragenkatalog, To-do-Logik, Basisdokumente), Verknüpfung mit Umweltmodul. Implement the following plan:

# CFF Backend Preparation Plan

## Overview
Prepare the `mock_data_v2/database_application_v2` backend service for deployment with:
1. Fix the POST endpoint bug in planner-output table
2. Merge two feature branches (context-path, kpi-productivity)
3. Create QA environment configuration alongside Dev
4. Update documentation

**Working Directory**: `/home/javiervel/clients/CFF/mock_data_v2/database_application_v2`
**Repository**: `bitbucket.org/bosenetdev/database-service` (separate from main CFF-project repo)
**Current Branch**: `master`
**Feature Branches**: `origin/feature/context-path`, `origin/feature/kpi-productivity`
**Project Context**: Parent project at `/home/javiervel/clients/CFF` (for reference to related services)

---

## Goal 1: Fix planner_output POST Endpoint

### Problem Identified
The POST endpoint at `/api/v1/planner-output/` fails with SQL syntax error:
```
syntax error at or near ":"
[SQL: INSERT INTO planner_output (...) VALUES (..., :assignments::jsonb) ...]
```

The issue is in `api/routers/planner_output.py:202` - the `::jsonb` cast doesn't work with asyncpg's positional parameter system.

### Fix Required
**File**: `api/routers/planner_output.py`

Change the INSERT query from using `:assignments::jsonb` to a compatible approach:
- Option A: Remove the `::jsonb` cast since the JSON string will be auto-converted
- Option B: Use `CAST(:assignments AS jsonb)` syntax

### Assessment: planner_output Table Design

**Current Schema** (good design):
| Column | Type | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| output_id | SERIAL | Primary key |
| plan_id | VARCHAR(50) | Unique solver session ID |
| status | VARCHAR(20) | PENDING/FEASIBLE/OPTIMAL/INFEASIBLE/ERROR |
| assignments | JSONB | Array of {request_id, resource_id, start, end, duration_hours} |
| num_requests, num_resources, num_violations | INTEGER | Metadata |
| solve_time_seconds | DECIMAL | Performance tracking |

**Recommendation**: Table design is efficient. The JSONB assignments array is appropriate for:
- Storing complete solver output in one row
- Quick retrieval by plan_id
- No need for separate assignments join table

**Deprecation Strategy** (future consideration):
- Add `expires_at` column for automatic cleanup
- Create index on `created_at` for range queries
- Consider partitioning by month if data grows large

---

## Goal 2: Merge Feature Branches

### Branch 1: `feature/context-path`
**Purpose**: Add ROOT_PATH support for `/dev/ai` prefix

**Changes**:
- `api/config.py`: Add `ROOT_PATH` setting (default: `/dev/ai`)
- `api/main.py`: Create `main_app` gateway that mounts `app` at ROOT_PATH
- `docker-compose.yml`: Add ROOT_PATH environment variable
- `docker/Dockerfile.api`: Update health check and gunicorn command
- `scripts/health-check.sh`: Update URLs to include `/dev/ai` prefix

**Merge Order**: Merge FIRST (foundation for both environments)

### Branch 2: `feature/kpi-productivity`
**Purpose**: Add KPI calculation endpoints

**Changes**:
- `api/kpi.py`: New 6,235-line KPI module with 7 endpoint groups:
  - Productivity KPIs
  - Vacation and Absence KPIs
  - External vs Internal Resource KPIs
  - Stability and Continuity KPIs
  - Function and Competency KPIs
  - Geographic and Travel KPIs
  - Coverage and Demand KPIs
- `api/main.py`: Import and include KPI router

**Important**: Does NOT include context-path changes - needs manual merge

### KPI-to-planner_output Integration Assessment

The KPI service currently queries the `assignments` table (external DB):
```python
# From api/kpi.py
query = text("SELECT resource_id, SUM(...) FROM assignments WHERE ...")
result = await db.execute(query, ...)
```

**To switch to planner_output**:
1. The `planner_output.assignments` JSONB contains the same structure
2. Would need to unnest JSONB array for aggregation queries
3. Example integration approach:
```sql
SELECT
  a->>'resource_id' as resource_id,
  SUM((a->>'duration_hours')::float) as total_hours
FROM planner_output, jsonb_array_elements(assignments) a
WHERE plan_id = :selected_plan_id
GROUP BY a->>'resource_id'
```

**Decision**: Add NEW KPI endpoints that read from `planner_output` to compare proposed vs actual.

This means:
- **Existing KPIs** (`/api/v1/pre-kpi/*`): Continue reading from `assignments` table (actual/confirmed)
- **New Planner KPIs** (`/api/v1/planner-kpi/*`): Read from `planner_output.assignments` JSONB (proposed)

New endpoints to create in `api/kpi.py` or new `api/planner_kpi.py`:
- `GET /planner-kpi/productivity/{plan_id}` - Productivity from specific plan
- `GET /planner-kpi/coverage/{plan_id}` - Coverage metrics from plan
- `GET /planner-kpi/compare/{plan_id}` - Compare plan assignments vs actual assignments

This allows frontend to:
1. See current state (actual KPIs from assignments)
2. Preview proposed plan impact (planner KPIs from planner_output)
3. Compare difference before approving a plan

### Merge Strategy
1. Checkout master: `git checkout master`
2. Merge context-path: `git merge origin/feature/context-path`
3. Merge kpi-productivity: `git merge origin/feature/kpi-productivity`
4. Resolve conflicts in `api/main.py` (combine both changes)
5. Apply planner_output fix
6. Test locally before pushing

---

## Goal 3: Create QA Environment

### Current State
- **Dev**: Port 8002, `/dev/ai` path, external DB user `cffdev`
- **QA**: Needs port 8003, `/qa/ai` path, external DB user `cffqa`

### Required Files

#### 1. `.env.qa` (new file)
```env
ROOT_PATH=/qa/ai
API_PORT=8003
DB_PORT=5436
POSTGRES_DATA_PATH=./data/postgres/qa

# Internal DB (same structure, different data path)
INTERNAL_DB_HOST=postgres-qa
INTERNAL_DB_PORT=5432
INTERNAL_DB_NAME=cff_planning
INTERNAL_DB_USER=cff_app_user
INTERNAL_DB_PASSWORD=changeme

# External DB (QA database)
EXTERNAL_DB_HOST=150.241.245.65
EXTERNAL_DB_PORT=5434
EXTERNAL_DB_NAME=cff
EXTERNAL_DB_USER=cffqa
EXTERNAL_DB_PASSWORD=cffqa
```

#### 2. `docker-compose.qa.yml` (new file)
Create separate compose file for QA with:
- Service names: `postgres-qa`, `api-qa`
- Container names: `cff_postgres_qa`, `cff_api_qa`
- Network: `cff_network_qa`
- Volume: `cff_postgres_data_qa`
- Port mappings: 8003:8000, 5436:5432

#### 3. `docker/Dockerfile.api.qa` (new file)
Copy from Dockerfile.api with health check using `/qa/ai/health`

### Deployment Approach

**Recommended**: Separate docker-compose files with shared Dockerfiles

```bash
# Deploy Dev
docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml --env-file .env up -d

# Deploy QA
docker-compose -f docker-compose.qa.yml --env-file .env.qa up -d
```

**Alternative**: Single docker-compose with profiles (more complex)

### Environment Isolation Notes
- Both environments share the same **external** database server (150.241.245.65) but different users/ports
- Each environment has its own **internal** PostgreSQL container (separate data)
- Internal DB tables (constraints, parameters) will need separate seeding per environment
- No automatic sync between Dev and QA internal DBs

## Implementation Order

### Phase 1: Fix and Merge (Local)
1. Fix planner_output.py POST bug
2. Merge feature/context-path to master
3. Merge feature/kpi-productivity to master
4. Resolve conflicts (main.py needs both changes)
5. Test POST endpoint: `curl -X POST localhost:8002/dev/ai/api/v1/planner-output/`
6. Add planner KPI endpoints (new file or extend kpi.py)
   - `/api/v1/planner-kpi/productivity/{plan_id}`
   - `/api/v1/planner-kpi/coverage/{plan_id}`
   - `/api/v1/planner-kpi/compare/{plan_id}`

### Phase 2: Create QA Environment
1. Create .env.qa file
2. Create docker-compose.qa.yml
3. Create Dockerfile.api.qa
4. Test QA deployment locally: `docker-compose -f docker-compose.qa.yml --env-file .env.qa up -d`

### Phase 3: Test Both Environments
1. Verify Dev at `localhost:8002/dev/ai/health`
2. Verify QA at `localhost:8003/qa/ai/health`
3. Test KPI endpoints on both
4. Test planner-output POST on both

### Phase 4: Documentation
1. Update README.md with v2.3 changes
2. Update API_ENDPOINTS.md with KPI section
3. Update DEPLOYMENT_GUIDE.md with multi-env instructions

### Phase 5: Commit and Push
1. Commit all changes with descriptive message
2. Push to origin/master

# Test POST endpoint

I need you to check the current state from the solver workflow and in a perspective from multi-user. There should be 2 workflows, first explore, plan and then we execute. 

Workflow 1: Triggering the solver. 
1.- trigger solver using a user_id; If user doesnt exists it simply takes the global values from global user. 
2.- If user exists it takes the constraints values, objectives hierarchies and weights from that specific user. 
3.- The solver runs in that user context, when finishing it saves the plan under that same user ID. 

Workflow 2: Initializing a user with API /api/v1/users/{user_id}/initialize
1.- Tables from seed are copied (they should persist, not being temporary, if they are in teh docker volume, or persisted through the folder ./data I need to know that, so we can also plan how to persist or do migrations after changes in DB)
2.- When fetching any constraint (except law constraints), objectoves values, weights or priorities it should be done from a user context (also falling in global if user doesnt exists or if user is not passed).

1.content:
#!/bin/bash

echo "chmod -x deploy.sh"

# --- 1. Git Pull and Validation ---
echo "--- Checking for updates from Bitbucket ---"
GIT_OUTPUT=$(git pull 2>&1)
GIT_EXIT_CODE=$?

echo "$GIT_OUTPUT"

GIT_LOG_1=$(git log -1)
echo "###########################################"
echo "Last commit: $GIT_LOG_1"
echo "###########################################"

echo "chmod +x deploy.sh"

if [ $GIT_EXIT_CODE -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "------------------------------------------------"
    echo "❌ ERROR: Git pull failed. Deployment aborted."
    echo "Please check your SSH connection or local changes."
    echo "------------------------------------------------"
    exit 1
fi

# --- 2. Argument and Confirmation Logic ---

2.content:
if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "❌ Error: No environment specified."
    echo "Usage: ./deploy.sh -dev | -qa | -uat | -dev -qa -uat"
    exit 1
fi

echo "------------------------------------------------"
echo "ATTENTION: You are about to deploy to: $@"
echo "This will DELETE database folders and REBUILD images."
echo "------------------------------------------------"
read -p "Are you sure you want to proceed? (y/n): " CONFIRM

if [[ ! $CONFIRM =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
    echo "🚫 Deployment cancelled by user."
    exit 0
fi

# --- 3. Deployment Functions ---

3.content:
# --- 3. Deployment Functions ---

deploy_dev() {
    echo "🚀 Starting DEV Deployment..."
    docker compose -f docker-compose.yml down -v
    sudo rm -rf ./data/postgres
    docker compose -f docker-compose.yml build --no-cache
    docker compose -f docker-compose.yml --env-file .env up -d
    echo "✅ DEV Deployment Finished."
}

deploy_qa() {
    echo "🚀 Starting QA Deployment..."
    docker compose -f docker-compose.qa.yml down -v
    sudo rm -rf ./data/postgres_qa
    docker compose -f docker-compose.qa.yml build --no-cache
    docker compose -f docker-compose.qa.yml --env-file .env.qa up -d
    echo "✅ QA Deployment Finished."
}

deploy_uat() {
    echo "🚀 Starting UAT Deployment..."
    docker compose -f docker-compose.uat.yml down -v
    sudo rm -rf ./data/postgres_uat
    docker compose -f docker-compose.uat.yml build --no-cache

4.content:
docker compose -f docker-compose.uat.yml --env-file .env.uat up -d
    echo "✅ UAT Deployment Finished."
}

# --- 4. Execution Loop ---
for arg in "$@"
do
    case $arg in
        -dev)
            deploy_dev
            ;;
        -qa)
            deploy_qa
            ;;
        -uat)
            deploy_uat
            ;;
        *)
            echo "❓ Unknown argument: $arg"
            exit 1
            ;;
    esac
done

echo "🎉 All requested deployments are complete."

**HNSW tuning parameters:**

| Parameter | Default | Effect |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| `m` | 16 | Graph connectivity. Higher = better recall, more memory |
| `ef_construction` | 128 | Build quality. Higher = better graph, slower indexing |
| `ef_search` | 100 | Search quality. Higher = better recall, slower queries |

2.- Family name mapping - The system doesnt have a resource id, it is relying in the resource family name to make the mapping. The solver uses the name to find absences related to that resource, its the only way that it can know resources absneces.

The first one. Identify the Category and qualification match constraint. Right now the solver matches resources to demands by using the categories and demands. A category has the priority, there is even a pre-filtering of categories to save space and memory. The hierarchy is: First match category then match qualification. There is something that is not yet implemented in the planner: 
The matching of category should happen by priority, when fetching the resource, the resource has a collection of categories as an object/array, whatever is in the first position has the highest priority and the priority decreases for the furthes position. Here is an example: 
For this resoruce, look at the categories: 
http://localhost:8003/qa/ai/api/v1/resources
[Pasted text #1 +23 lines]

See that there is an array with categories, the first one has the highest priority and the solver should try to accomodate the resource using that category first, then the next one and then the next one. 

here is the ticket: 
There where many time where the solver didnt use the Prioritized category of a ressource, but instead a category with much lower priority. For example a ressource has as highest category 'Contremaitre' and as lowest category 'Monteur VF'. Now for a month plan, the ressource was always assigned as Monteur VF, even though there was the option to put it as Contremaitre.
All in all, it seems that this prioritization of categories is not respected well enough

Check if there is an objective by priority or weight that we could make use of, or if there should be a new priority/objectove with weights as well. Maybe as it is rigid and it should always be like this it should just be another parameter implemented, but I need your help deciding this. 

As extra information here is another related ticket that you may have to considere as well for this fix: 
It happens, that a ressource is sent to a Demand with the same BSA-ID (that means its the same construction site) multiple times in a row in a week. This is a good thing, and should be the goal. But the solver also changed up the categories for the ressource within that same BSA-ID during the week. So the first three shifts it was assigned as Monteur VF and the fourth shift it was assigned as 'Chef/fe d'équipe', even though there still was a demand for Monteur VF that day. The plan should always try to keep the same ressource in the same category on the same BSA-ID in the following shifts if possible.

Here is a requirement where I need help implementing. Check the solver current flow, there is a part where it fetches data from assignments, this assignments essentially block resources availability and they can be filtered form teh payload. The current system allows for calendar types in the payload. If a calendar type is present that means that assignments in that category are filtered, meaning that those assignments are no longer valid, therfore we are replanning those entries. 

I need you to understand that flow logic. Here is a new logic that I need to implement. Inside assignments table I need to always enforce a calendar_type of assignments which actually are absences, I have a list that I need to hardcode in solver_v2/config.py:
[Pasted text #1 +9 lines]

This current enforcement would work if in the payload the parameter "lock_allocations" is true, but that is exactly why we need to enforce this, because absences should always block availability, regardless. Besides the hardcoded table, you must know that the absences are then also implemented in the absences table: 
/api/v1/absences/
http://localhost:8003/qa/ai/docs

So why not looking up the absences table instead? 
The issue is that at this point of development all absences are in assignments table. And there is already logic across teh whole pipeline that looks through the assignment table, so maybe is easier to use the hardcoded list and implement a mechanism to always enforce those assignments that match that calendar_type from the list. 

You must also know that eventualy assignments will only contain assignemnts and absences will strictly only be in the absences table. 

With this informaiton I expect you to understand the requirement, the issue and the long term plan and decide what is better according to the current implemented pipeline.

Then understand this requirement ticket: 
 CT and RT are not only from 24 Uhr to 24 Uhr, but can be also from 12 to 12 or 13 to 13. The planner never respected that, and always put it from 24 to 24. This has a use case, because for example a ressource could work a night shift after a CT day that goes from 12 to 12 (because from 12 to Night shift start there can be enough rest time), whereas a ressource that has a CT from 24 to 24 cant have a night shift directly after it.
